title: Local Property Tax (LPT) — Revenue
content:
Local Property Tax (LPT) is an annual self-assessed tax charged on the market value of residential properties in Ireland. It was introduced in 2013 and is administered by Revenue.

Who pays LPT
You are liable for LPT if you own a residential property in Ireland on 1 November each year. This includes owner-occupiers, landlords, holders of long leases (over 20 years), and local authorities. If you own multiple properties, you pay LPT on each one.

How LPT is calculated
LPT is based on your property's market value as at 1 November 2021 (the current valuation date). Properties fall into valuation bands, and a fixed rate of 0.1029% applies to the mid-point of each band up to €1.05 million. For properties valued above €1.05 million, Revenue calculates LPT differently — you pay on the actual value above €1.05 million.

Valuation bands and annual charges (2022 onward)
- Band 1: up to €200,000 — €90 per year
- Band 2: €200,001–€262,500 — €225 per year
- Band 3: €262,501–€350,000 — €315 per year
- Band 4: €350,001–€437,500 — €405 per year
- Band 5: €437,501–€525,000 — €495 per year
- Band 6: €525,001–€612,500 — €585 per year
- Band 7: €612,501–€700,000 — €675 per year
- Band 8: €700,001–€787,500 — €765 per year
- Band 9: €787,501–€875,000 — €855 per year
- Band 10: €875,001–€962,500 — €945 per year
- Band 11: €962,501–€1,050,000 — €1,035 per year
- Properties over €1.05 million are assessed at actual value

Local authority variations
Local authorities can vary LPT rates for properties in their area by up to 15% above or below the national rate. Check your local authority's current rate.

How to pay
You can pay LPT in one of several ways:
1. Annual debit authority (single bank debit) — deducted on 21 March
2. Monthly direct debit — spread across the year starting January
3. Deduction at source through employer or DSP
4. Online payment (credit or debit card) through myAccount or ROS
5. Payments through An Post

Filing and payment dates
- Annual debit authority: pay by 1 January of the tax year
- Online payment: pay by 1 January
- Phased payments: deductions begin in January
- Deduction at source: must be arranged by November of the prior year

Exemptions
The following properties are exempt from LPT:
- Properties built but not yet occupied, where they were not liable on 1 November 2021
- Properties with significant pyrite or mica damage certified by a competent professional
- Properties that are not suitable for use as a dwelling
- Some charity and religious properties

Deferrals
If you have a low income, you may defer paying LPT until you sell or transfer the property. Deferral is available if your gross income is below €18,000 (single person) or €30,000 (couple). Interest of 4% per year accrues on deferred amounts. You must still file your LPT return even if you defer payment.

LPT and rental properties
Landlords can claim LPT as a deductible expense for income tax purposes. Owner-occupiers cannot deduct LPT against income tax.

Revaluations
Properties are revalued periodically for LPT purposes. The current valuation date is 1 November 2021. Any revaluation date will be announced by Revenue in advance.

title: Universal Social Charge (USC) — Revenue
content:
Universal Social Charge (USC) is a tax on gross income — the total income you earn before any deductions. It is charged alongside income tax and PRSI, and is collected through PAYE if you are an employee.

USC rates 2025
USC is charged at different rates on different portions of income:
- 0.5%: on the first €12,012
- 2%: on €12,013 to €27,382
- 3%: on €27,383 to €70,044
- 8%: on income above €70,044

Self-employed people with non-PAYE income over €100,000 pay an additional 3% surcharge on the amount above €100,000.

Who pays USC
USC applies to individuals with total gross income over €13,000 per year. If your total income is €13,000 or less, you are exempt from USC entirely.

Exemptions and reduced rates
You are exempt from USC if:
- Your total income for the year is €13,000 or less
- You hold a full medical card and your total income is €60,000 or less (reduced rate applies — all income taxed at 2%)
- You are receiving certain DSP payments (these payments are exempt from USC)

Reduced rate for medical card holders
If you hold a full medical card and earn €60,000 or less per year, all your income is taxed at 2% USC instead of the standard rates. This reduced rate also applies if your spouse or civil partner holds the medical card and you are jointly assessed.

Income not subject to USC
The following are not counted for USC:
- Department of Social Protection payments (e.g., Jobseeker's, Carer's)
- Capital gains
- Deposit interest subject to DIRT
- Certain EU/UN allowances

USC on employment income
If you are a PAYE employee, USC is deducted from your pay each pay period. Your employer uses the Revenue Payroll Notification (RPN) to calculate the correct amount. You do not need to take any action unless your personal circumstances change.

USC for self-employed
Self-employed people pay USC as part of their annual self-assessment (Form 11). Preliminary tax must be paid by 31 October and includes a USC estimate for the current year.

Checking your USC
You can view your USC deductions for the current and previous years through myAccount at revenue.ie. The Employment Detail Summary shows your total gross income and USC paid each year.

title: myAccount — Revenue's Online Self-Service Portal
content:
myAccount is Revenue's secure online self-service portal for PAYE employees and individuals. It allows you to manage your tax record, claim tax credits and reliefs, and view your tax history without needing to contact Revenue directly.

What you can do in myAccount
- View and update your tax credits and rate bands
- Claim tax credits (medical expenses, rent tax credit, flat rate expenses, etc.)
- Review your tax for any of the previous 4 years (and claim a refund if you overpaid)
- View your Employment Detail Summary (replaces the old P60)
- Access and submit a Form 12 (income tax return for PAYE employees with minor additional income)
- Manage your Local Property Tax (LPT)
- View correspondence from Revenue
- Update your personal details (address, bank account for refunds)
- Register for or deregister from PAYE
- View your Revenue Payroll Notifications (RPNs)

How to register for myAccount
1. Go to revenue.ie and select 'myAccount'
2. Click 'Register' and enter your PPSN, date of birth, and phone number
3. You will receive a verification code by SMS or post
4. Once verified, set a password and security questions

Signing in
You can sign in to myAccount using:
- PPSN and password
- MyGovID (the government's verified identity service)

MyGovID provides a higher level of identity verification and is required for some services. You can register for MyGovID at mygov.ie.

Claiming tax credits through myAccount
To claim a tax credit:
1. Log in to myAccount
2. Select 'Review your tax' for the relevant year
3. Under 'Tax credits and reliefs', add the appropriate credit
4. Submit the claim
Refunds are paid directly to your bank account within 5 working days once approved.

Employment Detail Summary
The Employment Detail Summary replaced the P60 from 2019 onward. It shows your annual gross pay, income tax, USC, and PRSI for each employment. Access it through myAccount under 'Pay and File'.

MyEnquiries
If you need to contact Revenue directly, use MyEnquiries within myAccount. This is a secure messaging service — select the category of your query and submit. Typical response times are 5-10 working days.

Common issues
If you are locked out of myAccount, you can reset your password using your PPSN, date of birth, and phone number. If you cannot reset online, contact Revenue through MyEnquiries or call the PAYE helpline on (01) 738 3636.

title: Emergency Tax — Revenue
content:
Emergency tax is applied when your employer does not receive a Revenue Payroll Notification (RPN) for you. Without an RPN, your employer does not know what tax credits or rate bands to apply to your pay, so they deduct tax at emergency rates — which are significantly higher than normal.

Why you might be on emergency tax
- Starting a new job and not yet registered with Revenue
- Starting a first job in Ireland and not registered for PAYE
- Returning to work after a long break
- Revenue has suspended your tax credits (for example, due to arrears)
- Your employer has not yet downloaded your RPN

Emergency tax rates
If your employer does not have an RPN:
- Weeks 1-4: you are taxed at the standard rate (20%) with no tax credits
- Week 5 onwards: you are taxed at the higher rate (40%) with no tax credits

As a result, emergency tax can be significantly more than you should be paying.

How to stop emergency tax
Register your new employment with Revenue as soon as you start work:
1. Log in to myAccount at revenue.ie
2. Select 'Jobs and Pensions'
3. Add your new employer using their tax registration number (ask your employer for this)
4. Revenue will automatically issue an updated RPN to your employer
5. Your employer will apply the correct tax credits from the next pay run

First job in Ireland
If this is your first job in Ireland, you need to register for PAYE through myAccount. You will need a PPSN (Personal Public Service Number). If you do not have a PPSN, apply through your local Intreo office or online.

Claiming back emergency tax
If you paid emergency tax, you are entitled to a refund of any overpaid tax. To claim:
1. Log in to myAccount
2. Select 'Review your tax' for the relevant year
3. Revenue will calculate whether you overpaid and issue a refund

Refunds are paid to your bank account and typically take 5 working days.

Contact Revenue
If your employer cannot obtain an RPN for you and you believe your tax credits should be in place, contact Revenue through MyEnquiries or call the PAYE Helpline on (01) 738 3636.

title: Health Expenses Tax Relief — Revenue
content:
You can claim income tax relief on health expenses that you paid for yourself, your spouse or civil partner, or any other person. The relief is 20% of the amount you paid, meaning Revenue will refund €20 for every €100 of eligible expenses.

Who can claim
Any PAYE employee or self-assessed taxpayer can claim health expenses relief. You do not need to have private health insurance — the relief applies to out-of-pocket costs not reimbursed by insurance or otherwise.

Eligible health expenses
You can claim relief on the following:
- Doctor and GP visit fees (including private consultants)
- Dental treatment (non-routine: crowns, fillings, orthodontics for clinical need — routine check-ups are NOT eligible)
- Optician fees (eye examinations and prescription glasses are NOT eligible)
- Hospital stays and treatment
- Prescribed medications (you need a prescription, over-the-counter medicines are not eligible)
- Physiotherapy, chiropody, occupational therapy
- Psychological counselling (licensed practitioners only)
- Diagnostic tests (X-rays, MRI, blood tests)
- Nursing home fees for yourself or a relative
- Medical appliances (hearing aids, insulin pumps, wheelchairs)
- Fertility treatment
- Gluten-free food for celiacs (with medical certificate)

Not eligible
- Routine dental check-ups and cleaning
- Cosmetic procedures
- Gym memberships or fitness programmes
- Vitamins or supplements without prescription
- Spectacles or contact lenses

How much can you claim
Relief is at 20% of qualifying expenses (not the marginal rate). There is no annual limit on the amount you can claim, but you must have receipts.

Nursing home expenses
Nursing home fees for yourself or a dependent qualify for relief at your marginal rate (20% or 40%), not just the standard rate. This is a significant saving for people with relatives in residential care.

How to claim
1. Log in to myAccount at revenue.ie
2. Select 'Review your tax' for the relevant year
3. Under 'Health', add the relevant expenses
4. Enter the amounts paid (not reimbursed by insurance)
5. Submit

Keep your receipts for 6 years — Revenue may ask to see them.

Claiming for previous years
You can claim health expenses for any of the previous 4 tax years. If you have never claimed before, you could receive a substantial refund.

Med 2 form
For dental expenses, you need a Med 2 form completed and signed by your dentist. For other health expenses, standard receipts are sufficient.

title: Home Carer Tax Credit — Revenue
content:
The Home Carer Tax Credit is available to married couples or civil partners where one spouse or partner stays at home (or works part-time) to care for a dependent person. For 2025, the credit is worth €1,800 per year, which reduces your income tax bill by €1,800.

Who qualifies
You can claim the Home Carer Tax Credit if:
- You are married or in a civil partnership assessed for tax jointly
- Your spouse or civil partner cares full-time or part-time for a dependent person
- The dependent person is: a child for whom Child Benefit is paid, a person over 65, or a person with a permanent disability requiring full-time care

The dependent person must live with the couple. It does not apply to the care of a non-dependent adult unless they are incapacitated.

Income limit for the home carer
The home carer can earn up to €7,200 per year and still receive the full credit. If their income is between €7,200 and €10,800, a reduced credit applies. Above €10,800, the credit is not available.

Calculating the reduced credit
The credit is reduced by half the amount by which the home carer's income exceeds €7,200. For example:
- Home carer earns €8,000 → excess is €800 → credit reduced by €400 → credit available: €1,400
- Home carer earns €9,000 → excess is €1,800 → credit reduced by €900 → credit available: €900
- Home carer earns €10,800 → no credit available

How to claim
1. Log in to myAccount
2. Select 'Manage my tax 2025' (or the relevant year)
3. Under 'Tax credits and reliefs', select 'Home Carer Tax Credit'
4. Enter the home carer's income and details of the dependent

Other credits available with home carer status
You cannot claim the Home Carer Tax Credit and the Incapacitated Child Tax Credit in the same year for the same dependent. You also cannot claim the Home Carer Tax Credit and the Increased Standard Rate Band (double-income credit) for the same tax year — Revenue will apply whichever gives the greater benefit.

Amount for 2024 and 2025
The Home Carer Tax Credit was €1,800 for 2024 and remains €1,800 for 2025.

title: Remote Working Tax Relief (e-Worker Expenses) — Revenue
content:
If you work from home as part of your employment, you may be able to claim tax relief on the costs of running your home office. Revenue refers to this as e-worker expenses or remote working relief.

What you can claim
Revenue allows a deduction for additional costs you incur because you work from home. This is calculated as a percentage of your actual household bills:
- Electricity: 30% of the electricity costs relating to the days you work from home
- Heating: 30% of the heating costs relating to days worked from home
- Broadband: 30% of your broadband bill relating to working days

How the calculation works
Revenue uses a flat rate method to simplify the calculation. For each day you work from home, you can claim €3.20 in respect of heating and electricity costs combined, and a proportional share of your broadband costs. You can claim the actual expenses instead if you have precise records.

Working from home arrangement
The relief only applies if you are working from home as part of a formal arrangement with your employer — not just occasionally checking emails. Revenue expects the employer to confirm or permit the arrangement.

How to claim
1. Log in to myAccount
2. Select 'Review your tax' for the relevant year
3. Under 'Employment', select 'Remote Working'
4. Enter the number of days worked at home, total electricity/heating bills for the year, and your broadband cost
5. Revenue will calculate the allowable deduction

Employer contributions
If your employer contributes to your home office costs, any amount up to €3.20 per day is tax-free. If they pay more than €3.20 per working-from-home day, the excess is taxable. You cannot double-claim: if your employer pays €3.20 per day tax-free, you cannot also claim the remote working tax relief for those same days.

Keeping records
Keep utility bills and a record of the number of days you worked from home for 6 years in case Revenue requests verification.

Capital expenses
Furniture and equipment for a home office are not deductible for employees (as opposed to self-employed people). Revenue's e-worker scheme only covers running costs (electricity, heating, broadband).

title: Tuition Fee Relief (Third Level) — Revenue
content:
You can claim income tax relief on tuition fees you pay for an approved third-level course in Ireland or another EU/EEA country. The relief is available to the person who pays the fees — either the student or a parent.

How much you can claim
Tax relief is at the standard rate (20%) on tuition fees. The relief applies to the portion of fees above a threshold of €3,000 per year (one claimant). The maximum fees you can claim on is €7,000 per student per year.

For the first student, no relief on the first €3,000. For second and subsequent students (paid by the same person in the same year), no threshold applies — you claim on the full amount.

Example
- You pay €5,000 fees for your child. Threshold: €3,000. Eligible amount: €2,000. Relief at 20%: €400 refund.
- You pay fees for two children: €5,000 each. For the first: €2,000 eligible → €400 refund. For the second: €5,000 fully eligible → €1,000 refund.

Eligible courses
The course must be:
- A full-time or part-time undergraduate or postgraduate degree at an approved institution
- Approved by the Minister for Finance
- At least 2 academic years in duration

Approved institutions include Irish universities (UCD, TCD, UCC, NUIG, DCU, UL), Institutes of Technology, and approved institutions in EU/EEA countries.

What is NOT eligible
- Exam fees and registration fees
- Accommodation or maintenance costs
- Foreign language courses
- One-year or short courses
- Online-only courses that are not part of an approved programme

Students on SUSI grants
If a student receives a SUSI grant that covers part of the fees, you can only claim tax relief on the portion of fees NOT covered by the grant.

How to claim
1. Log in to myAccount
2. Select 'Review your tax' for the relevant year
3. Under 'Education', select 'Tuition Fees'
4. Enter the name of the institution, the amount of fees paid, and confirm whether any grant was received
5. Submit

You can claim for the previous 4 tax years. Keep receipts from the college confirming fees paid.

title: Tax Credits Overview — Revenue
content:
Tax credits reduce the amount of income tax you pay. If your tax liability is €4,000 and you have credits of €3,750, you pay only €250. Most PAYE employees receive their tax credits automatically. You may be entitled to additional credits that need to be claimed through myAccount.

Standard tax credits (2025)
These credits apply to most PAYE employees:
- Personal Tax Credit: €1,875 (single person) or €3,750 (married/civil partner)
- PAYE Tax Credit: €1,875 (employees only — not available to self-employed)
- Earned Income Tax Credit: €1,875 (self-employed and proprietary directors — alternative to PAYE credit)

Credits you may need to claim
- Rent Tax Credit: €1,000 per year (single), €2,000 (couple) — for renters in private accommodation
- Medical Expenses Relief: 20% of eligible health costs not reimbursed by insurance
- Home Carer Tax Credit: €1,800 — for couples where one carer for dependants at home
- Single Person Child Carer Credit: €1,750 — for lone parents caring for a child
- Incapacitated Child Tax Credit: €3,500 — for parents of a permanently incapacitated child
- Dependent Relative Tax Credit: €245 — for taxpayers supporting a dependent relative
- Age Tax Credit: €245 (single) or €490 (couple) — for people aged 65 and over
- Blind Person's Tax Credit: €1,650 — for blind or visually impaired taxpayers
- Flat Rate Expenses: various amounts by occupation — covers tools, uniforms, etc.

Reliefs (reduce taxable income rather than tax directly)
- Tuition Fee Relief: 20% of eligible third-level fees above €3,000
- Pension contributions: contributions to an approved pension reduce taxable income at your marginal rate
- Employment expenses (actual, not flat rate): if your employer does not reimburse work-related expenses
- Cycle to Work scheme expenses

Rate bands (2025)
- Standard rate (20%): applies on income up to €44,000 (single), €53,000 (married, one income), or €88,000 (married, two incomes)
- Higher rate (40%): applies on income above those limits

How to check and claim
1. Log in to myAccount at revenue.ie
2. Select 'Manage my tax 2025'
3. Under 'Tax credits and reliefs', review what is currently applied
4. Add any credits you are entitled to but have not yet claimed
5. Revenue processes claims within 5 working days

You can claim for the previous 4 years — many people are entitled to refunds they have not yet claimed.

title: Employment Detail Summary (Replacement for P60) — Revenue
content:
The Employment Detail Summary replaced the P60 certificate from 2019 onwards. Under the enhanced PAYE system introduced in 2019, employers report pay and deductions in real time to Revenue. At year end, Revenue automatically produces an Employment Detail Summary for each employee — you no longer need to request a P60 from your employer.

What it shows
The Employment Detail Summary shows, for each employer in a given tax year:
- Total gross pay
- Income tax deducted
- USC (Universal Social Charge) deducted
- PRSI deducted (employee contribution)
- Any benefits-in-kind
- Employer's tax registration number

How to access it
1. Log in to myAccount at revenue.ie
2. Select 'Pay, pensions, profit' (or 'Pay and File')
3. Select 'Employment Detail Summary'
4. Choose the tax year

You can view and download your Employment Detail Summary for the current and previous years at any time.

Using it for proof of income
Many organisations — banks, mortgage lenders, rental applications, embassies — accept the Employment Detail Summary as proof of income or tax paid. Download the PDF from myAccount and share it as you would a P60.

Multiple employers
If you had more than one employer in a tax year, each employment appears as a separate entry on your Employment Detail Summary.

Errors on your Employment Detail Summary
If the figures appear incorrect (for example, if income is missing or the wrong tax was deducted), contact your employer first — they are responsible for submitting accurate payroll data. If your employer cannot resolve it, contact Revenue through MyEnquiries.

P45 — leaving a job
When you leave a job, your employer no longer issues a paper P45. Instead, Revenue updates your payroll record automatically. Your new employer downloads your RPN from Revenue, which includes your up-to-date tax credits and rate bands.

Form P21 — review of tax
If you want a formal statement showing whether you overpaid or underpaid tax in a given year, request a Form P21 (End of Year Statement) through myAccount. If Revenue calculates that you overpaid, a refund is issued to your bank account.
